excerpts.text: We must find a way for Christianity to exist in socialist China. Confrontation will only bring destruction.
The Three-Self principle — self-governance, self-support, self-propagation — is not a Communist invention. Missionaries themselves advocated it for a century.
I am often asked whether the Chinese church is free. I answer: freer than it was under missionary control, less free than it should be.
God's love is the central truth of Christianity. Not judgment, not punishment, not doctrine — love.
The cosmic Christ is at work beyond the boundaries of the church. God is not limited to those who call themselves Christian.
I chose to work within the system. Others chose prison. I do not judge them, and I ask them not to judge me.
When all the missionaries left in 1951, many predicted the death of Christianity in China. Instead it grew beyond anything the missionaries achieved.
The Chinese church must develop its own theology — not imported from America, not dictated by the state, but born from our own encounter with God.
At Nanjing Seminary we train pastors to serve both God and the Chinese people. These are not contradictory loyalties.
The Cultural Revolution destroyed everything visible about the church. But it could not destroy what was invisible — faith in human hearts.
I was ordained an Anglican bishop. I remain committed to the catholic tradition of the church while serving in a socialist context.
Wang Mingdao was a man of great integrity. We disagreed profoundly about strategy, but I never questioned his faith.
The registered church and the unregistered church are both the body of Christ. Our division grieves the Holy Spirit.
Missionaries brought the gospel to China. They also brought gunboats. We had to separate the two.
I have been called a collaborator and a hero, sometimes in the same sentence. History will judge. I did what I believed was right.
The Three-Self Movement kept churches open when the alternative was total destruction. That is not collaboration. That is survival.
China has more Christians today than the entire population of many European countries. The church did not die. It multiplied.
Theology must begin from reality, not from doctrine. The reality of China shapes the theology of the Chinese church.
I pray for unity between the house churches and the registered churches. We worship the same Lord.
My generation experienced the worst of foreign missions and the worst of domestic persecution. We survived both by the grace of God.
